payload.text: Build an app for giving advices on how to do 3d modeling (on Blender) and then 3d printing (On Bambu Lab P1S) . The app must allow the user to upload pictures, and select a zone to give advices and step by step on this specific zone only, while keeping the context of the full picture. The tone of the advices must be like a mentor or a teacher, professional.

**Define the Application's Scope**

I'm now focusing on defining the application's scope. The user needs a React web app to teach 3D modeling with Blender and 3D printing with the Bambu Lab P1S. I'm exploring features like image upload for reference, step-by-step tutorials, model downloads, and potentially user progress tracking.



**Elaborating the Use Cases**

I'm now diving deeper into the user experience. I'm imagining a workflow where a user uploads an image and then selects specific areas for tailored advice. The goal is to provide targeted help, maintaining the context of the whole image while explaining the intricacies of the selected zone. The tone will be that of a professional mentor, guiding the user in areas like topology and printability issues. I'm leaning toward a React 18+ stack with TypeScript, Tailwind CSS, and Google GenAI.


**Refining the Input Method**

I've realized that the initial idea of just a cropped section might be insufficient. Now, I'm thinking that sending the full image along with the cropped section allows the model to maintain the broader context, which will improve the quality of the insights. This is an improvement.


**Deciding the AI Model**

I've settled on `gemini-2.5-flash` for the image analysis. Its multimodal capabilities and speed are a good fit. I've drafted system instructions for prompt engineering, emphasizing my expertise in 3D modeling and printing. I'm focusing on providing context with both the full and selected images.

I'm now focusing on the application's state management. I've defined key variables like base64 image data, the selected region, the analysis results (in markdown), a loading state, and a history of interactions. Now I'm focused on the UI, refining how users will define the selection area with simple mouse interactions on the image.


**Constructing the Interface**

I'm currently building the UI for user interaction. I've designed a selection box using absolute positioning within the image container. My code converts screen coordinates to image coordinates for accurate cropping. I'm focusing on making the selection process intuitive. My goal is for the user to be able to drag a selection box over the parts of the image that they need assistance on.


**Finalizing the Visuals**

I've been working on the UI's final look. A dark theme with neutral blues seems best. The "Reset Selection" button is in place, and I'm ensuring the selection process is intuitive. I've also verified that the UI is in line with the established constraints, including React, TypeScript, Tailwind, and the Gemini API.


**Determining Cropping Logic**

I'm now focusing on calculating the precise cropping coordinates. The natural image dimensions may differ from the rendered size, so I need to compute the scaling ratio for accurate region selection. I'm also now going to use `gemini-2.5-flash` instead of `gemini-3-pro-preview` for speed and availability.

I've got the project architecture in place. I have defined the files and their purpose, and now I'm ready to begin the coding phase!


**Beginning the Implementation**

Now I'm planning to use the ResizeObserver API for handling window resizing.  This will help keep the selection box and image area correctly positioned. I'm focusing on crafting the mouse events to draw the selection box.  I'm also reviewing the system instruction for the Gemini prompt.

entries.description: App metadata definition.
HTML entry point with Tailwind CSS and fonts.
React application entry point.
Shared TypeScript types for the application.
Utilities for image processing and coordinate conversion.
Service for interacting with the Google GenAI API.
Component for displaying an image and selecting a crop zone.
Component to display the advice generated by Gemini.
Main application layout and state orchestration.

payload.text: adapt the tone of the advices, the audience is people with good knowledge of the basis, so it should focus on the advanced and specific parts and advices

**Adapting Advice Tone**

I'm now revising my approach to better target the intended audience. I'm shifting the focus of the AI advice. Instead of basic Blender and P1S instructions, it will now target users already well-versed in the fundamentals. The goal is to delve into advanced and specific aspects of each field, providing in-depth insights and strategies.


**Modifying System Instruction**

I'm currently working to modify the `SYSTEM_INSTRUCTION` variable within `services/geminiService.ts`. The aim is to refine the system's ability to offer more targeted advice. I need to ensure the instruction more effectively guides the AI to provide specific insights based on the images provided, making the advice more actionable for advanced users. The challenge is balancing specificity with adaptability for diverse user inquiries.


**Refining System Focus**

I'm now revising the system instruction to explicitly target an intermediate-to-advanced audience. My aim is to adjust the tone towards a professional, peer-to-peer expert consultation, rather than beginner-level teaching. I'm focusing on providing more advanced techniques for topology flow, shading, and non-destructive workflows, especially for Blender and the Bambu Lab P1S printer. This includes optimizing specific slicer settings for the P1S to help users troubleshoot and refine their outputs.


**Optimizing Print Parameters**

I'm now integrating insights on advanced slicer settings, like the wall vs. infill trade-off for strength, and specific support interface adjustments. This includes detailed advice on Bambu Lab P1S idiosyncrasies, such as AUX fan management, optimal print speeds for various filaments, and pressure advance/flow rate tuning. I'm focusing on providing expert guidance to help users diagnose and refine their prints based on visual cues.


**Targeting Advanced Techniques**

I'm now integrating highly technical insights. I'm focusing on providing specific solutions related to shading artifacts caused by N-pole disruption on curved surfaces. I'm also including advanced Blender strategies such as edge flow optimization for subdivision surfaces and non-destructive modifier stacks. Furthermore, I'm integrating specific Bambu Studio slicer settings for the P1S, like adjusting "Support Top Z Distance" for PETG and print orientation advice for layer adhesion, along with specific hardware considerations like AUX fan management.


**Analyzing and Refining Tone**

I'm now revising system instructions in `services/geminiService.ts` to remove any beginner-specific references. I'm also auditing hardcoded strings in `AdvicePanel.tsx` and `App.tsx` for tone appropriateness. Specifically, I'm adjusting "Ready to Mentor" to "Ready to Analyze" and maintaining the app name as "PolyPrint Mentor". I will focus on updating the tone of advice. I plan to also update the metadata if possible.


**Implementing Specialized Focus**

I'm now integrating comprehensive adjustments to the system instruction, specifically in `services/geminiService.ts`. I am refining the AI's capabilities to better provide expert-level technical consultations for both Blender and the Bambu Lab P1S. I am emphasizing advanced topics, such as modifier stacks and print optimization, and will skip basic instructions. The system will prioritize sophisticated techniques in topology, shading, and slicing for the target audience.


**Enhancing Print Analysis**

I'm now integrating highly technical insights. I'm focusing on specific Bambu Studio slicer parameters, like the choice between Arachne and Classic wall generators for thin walls. I'm also including detailed advice on cooling logic, such as part cooling vs. AUX fan, and speed/acceleration tuning tailored to small features versus structural integrity. The updated tone is aimed at senior engineers, emphasizing "why" and "how specifically" for authoritative, concise technical guidance.


**Analyzing Core Instructions**

I'm now revising the `SYSTEM_INSTRUCTION` in `services/geminiService.ts` to ensure it targets advanced users exclusively. I've updated the instruction to emphasize technical consultation for hard surface modeling and print optimization, explicitly mentioning Blender and the Bambu Lab P1S. My goal is to streamline the AI's focus on advanced topics and bypass any beginner-level explanations. I'm focusing on ensuring it aligns with expert requirements.


**Focusing Technical Insights**

I'm now integrating highly technical insights. I'm prioritizing the identification of complex topology issues like shading artifacts and specific print risks such as suction cups. I'm also now focusing on recommending advanced Blender workflows, like non-destructive modifier stacks and topology redirection strategies. Additionally, I am including specific Bambu Studio parameters for the P1S, like the Arachne wall generator for variable width walls and cooling logic for structural integrity and surface quality optimization.


**Refining System Prompts**

I've just finished revising the system instructions in `services/geminiService.ts` to focus entirely on advanced technical analysis and consultation. I've also updated `App.tsx` to set the default prompt to "Provide an expert technical analysis of this area for Blender topology and P1S manufacturing optimization." and changed the loading text in `AdvicePanel.tsx` to "Conducting Technical Analysis..." to maintain a professional tone. I've also updated the metadata.json, and the PolyPrint Mentor will be launched shortly.


**Implementing Final Touches**

I've made the final touches to the system's instructions and prompts to align with the desired expert tone. I've updated the `index.html` title to reflect the shift towards advanced users. I'm now certain that the core functionality is perfectly set to deliver detailed, technical analyses. The app is ready to launch.

Updated loading text and empty state text to sound more professional and technical.
Updated description to reflect expert-level tone.

payload.text:
allow the choice of language output between french and english, and the kind of advices they want among this list of profiles : C'est une excellente idĂ©e d'application, surtout avec ton profil technique (Blender, Unreal) et ton Ă©quipement (Bambu Lab P1S). Le fait de pouvoir changer de "persona" est crucial : on ne parle pas de la mĂŞme faĂ§on quand on debug un maillage non-manifold pour l'impression que quand on cherche l'inspiration artistique.

Voici 5 profils distincts que j'ai imaginĂ©s pour ton application, en tenant compte de ton matĂ©riel et de tes compĂ©tences :

    L'IngĂ©nieur Bambu (L'Expert Technique)

        Ton : PrĂ©cis, concis, orientĂ© "rĂ©solution de problĂ¨mes".

        Expertise : MaĂ®trise parfaite du Slicer (Bambu Studio), des contraintes physiques (porte-Ă -faux, tolĂ©rances, rĂ©sistance des matĂ©riaux).

        UtilitĂ© : Il analysera ton image pour te dire : "Attention, cette partie nĂ©cessitera des supports arborescents sur ta P1S" ou "Optimise l'orientation pour Ă©viter le warping". IdĂ©al pour la phase finale avant impression.

    Le Sculpteur NumĂ©rique (L'Artiste)

        Ton : CrĂ©atif, visuel, inspirant.

        Expertise : Blender (Sculpt mode), ZBrush, proportions, esthĂ©tique.

        UtilitĂ© : Il t'aidera Ă  interprĂ©ter une image 2D en volume. "Pour cette texture de pierre (monument historique), utilise telle brosse dans Blender pour donner du grain".

    Le Mentor PĂ©dagogue (Le Professeur)

        Ton : Encouragent, didactique, patient.

        Expertise : Enseignement de la modĂ©lisation 3D pas Ă  pas.

        UtilitĂ© : Parfait si tu t'attaques Ă  un style que tu maĂ®trises moins. Il dĂ©composera l'image en formes primitives simples (cubes, cylindres) pour dĂ©marrer ta modĂ©lisation.

    Le "MacGyver" du Prototypage (Le Maker)

        Ton : DĂ©contractĂ©, pragmatique, "Geek".

        Expertise : Impression rapide, itĂ©ration, astuces de code (OpenSCAD ou scripts Python dans Blender).

        UtilitĂ© : "T'embĂŞte pas Ă  modĂ©liser Ă§a en haute dĂ©f, fais un low-poly et active le 'Fuzzy Skin' dans Bambu Studio, Ă§a passera nickel."

    L'Archiviste 3D (L'Historien - SpĂ©cial pour toi)

        Ton : Formel, passionnĂ© par le dĂ©tail et l'authenticitĂ©.

        Expertise : Reconstruction historique, photogrammĂ©trie, architecture.

        UtilitĂ© : Il se concentrera sur la fidĂ©litĂ© par rapport Ă  l'image de rĂ©fĂ©rence, s'assurant que les proportions architecturales du monument sont respectĂ©es.

Currently, I'm translating persona descriptions into system instructions for Gemini and considering how each persona might deliver their analysis. I've been working on creating a map that links each persona to its system prompt and handles language instructions. Specifically, I'm defining system prompts for each persona, tailoring them for Gemini's optimal response. I'm focusing on "Engineer" (Bambu Studio, technical), "Artist" (Blender sculpting, aesthetic), "Teacher" (basic shapes, simple modifiers), "Maker" (speed, fuzzy skin, iteration), and "Historian" (accuracy, architectural details, context). I'm also preparing to add settings in `App.tsx` and planning language toggles and a persona selector.

**Organizing the User Interface**

I'm now determining how to integrate the language and persona selectors into the user interface. I'm thinking of placing these controls in the main action area near the "Analyze" button, to maintain a logical flow. The prompt mentioned language output and the kind of advice, so I'll position the selectors to align with this need. I'm focusing on ensuring the UI remains clean and intuitive.


**Prioritizing UI Integration**

I'm now determining the best location for the language and persona selectors. The "Session Settings" header seems cleaner for global settings. I'll update `App.tsx` to include these selectors. I will add a toolbar inside the `main` section, probably above the image workspace. I'm considering placing the selectors near the query input, which would allow for on-the-fly persona changes.


**Integrating Language Support**

I'm now focusing on integrating the language and persona selectors into the header for an intuitive user experience. I'm considering mobile responsiveness and the best placement for the dropdowns. Given that the personas have French names, I'm thinking about localizing the labels to match the selected language, or at least provide translations. I will include a global configuration for "App Configuration". I will replace the current settings with a language selector and persona selector in the header, or a dedicated "Settings" bar above the image.


**Defining Language Support**

I'm now integrating the French-English language toggle by creating a translation map and modifying the `Language` type in `types.ts`. I've added an `interface` to handle UI label translations dynamically. This helps to support the prompt's request for language output. I'm focusing on making the language selector intuitive for the user, and ensuring the interface is adaptable. The UI labels are staying in English for consistency.


**Implementing Dynamic System Prompts**

I'm now generating a `SYSTEM_INSTRUCTION` dynamically, taking into account user-selected language and persona. I'm adding `language` and `persona` state variables with UI components for selection in `App.tsx`. I've begun crafting the system prompts, ensuring they reflect the essence of the French persona descriptions. I'll translate these descriptions to ensure consistent role interpretation for the AI, focusing on the dark theme aesthetics for the dropdowns.

The following changes implement language selection (English/French) and the 5 specific advice personas requested.

# Updated files:

## types.ts
Added `Language` type and `Persona` enum to define the new configuration options.

## services/geminiService.ts
Updated `analyzeImageZone` to accept `language` and `persona`. The `SYSTEM_INSTRUCTION` is now dynamically generated based on the selected persona, mapping the specific French descriptions provided in the prompt to technical AI instructions. It also strictly enforces the output language.

## App.tsx
Added state for `language` and `persona`. Updated the UI to include a configuration toolbar in the header allowing users to switch between languages and advice profiles. The analysis call now passes these parameters to the service.

payload.text: Add zoom and pan functionality to the ImageAnnotator component, allowing users to inspect fine details of the image before making a selection. Allow the selection to be a square, a circle or a lasso selection

**Enhancing ImageAnnotator**

I've been focusing on adding zoom and pan to the `ImageAnnotator` component.  It's exciting! I'm also exploring different selection shapes â€“ rectangles, ellipses, and freehand lasso selections. I'm prioritizing user experience and efficient interaction.


**Adapting Selection Shapes**

I'm pondering the implications of adding circle and lasso selections to `ImageAnnotator`. My initial thought is to update `types.ts` to accommodate these new shapes, but I'm unsure if that's the best approach. The `cropImage` utility in `utils/canvasUtils.ts` currently works with rectangles. I'm exploring how to handle non-rectangular crops, and whether to send the actual masked image or just the bounding box to the Gemini image analysis service. I'm leaning toward the latter for simplicity, especially since the APIs typically accept rectangular images. The user's prompt or the context can explain the shape of the selection.


**Evaluating Gemini Integration**

My latest thinking centers on integrating the lasso selection with Gemini. I confirmed that `services/geminiService.ts` currently sends a base64-encoded cropped image. I'm updating `utils/canvasUtils.ts` to accommodate lasso selections, including calculating the bounding box.  I plan to mask pixels outside the lasso before sending the rectangular crop to Gemini. This simplifies the AI interaction, but respects the user's intent. My progress is focused on maintaining API compatibility.


**Integrating Shape Handling**

I'm now focusing on integrating the different selection shapes. For rectangles, I'll keep the current implementation. For circles and lasso selections, I'll need to draw the respective paths for image clipping before rendering. I'm also considering the best way to handle zoom and pan: a dedicated toolbar or using standard web conventions like scroll-to-zoom and space+drag for panning. This means updating coordinate conversions to account for the image's transform.  I am thinking a toolbar would be best to choose the selection tool; square, circle, or lasso.


**Designing Tool State**

I'm now deep-diving into the state management for my tools. Thinking of the logic, a `activeTool` state seems like a clear way to organize drawing: rectangles will use a start/current point approach, while circles use a bounding box with the option to change the center. Lasso will involve adding points on each mouse move until the mouse up is detected. I need to make sure the user can consistently draw with these shapes. Zoom/Pan will be next.


**Adjusting Coordinate Transformations**

I'm now focused on the coordinate transformations needed for zoom and pan.  I've nailed down the logic: I map client event coordinates to container-relative coordinates, and then apply the inverse transform based on the zoom level and pan position.  This allows me to determine the corresponding image coordinates.  I'm also setting up my selection shapes as a discriminated union in `types.ts`, and considering the file modifications. The next task is to update `canvasUtils.ts` to support different shapes.


**Finalizing Shape Types**

I'm solidifying the types for shape selections. In `types.ts`, I've added a `SelectionTool` enum for 'RECTANGLE', 'CIRCLE', and 'LASSO'. My `Selection` type now uses a discriminated union for clear data structure: rectangles define x, y, width, height; circles also, while lasso is defined by an array of points. I'm preparing to update `canvasUtils.ts` to accommodate these.


**Rendering Selection Logic**

I'm now focusing on how I'll handle drawing and clipping for each shape in `canvasUtils.ts`.  Rectangles are the simplest; Circles will use `ctx.ellipse` for clipping, and Lasso will use `ctx.beginPath`, move to each point, and then `ctx.clip`. I need to shift the drawing so the bounding box is at the origin. I also need to render the image in `components/ImageAnnotator.tsx`, where I've added state for `scale`, `pan`, and `tool`. I'll add a toolbar with zoom and tool buttons. I'll make the interactions handle mouse wheel zoom, and spacebar+drag pan. The overlay will be inside the image container.


**Optimizing Coordinate Conversions**

I'm now deep-diving into the coordinate transformations. I've realized I need to convert between screen, image-space, and natural image sizes. I'll store all selections in "image-space," relative to the unscaled image. The drawing code will convert mouse events to image coordinates, accounting for zoom and pan.  I also need to scale the final output to the natural image size for analysis.  Now focusing on updating `App.tsx` and handling selection state.


**Defining Coordinate Spaces**

My main focus is finalizing the coordinate spaces. `ImageAnnotator` will now return selections in the image element's coordinate space (0 to image width/height). `App.tsx` handles scaling to the natural image size. I've been reworking the implementation details for `ImageAnnotator.tsx`. The container will have `overflow: hidden` and `relative` positioning. A transform layer will use `transform-origin: 0 0` and `transform: translate(...) scale(...)`. Finally, the container will attach events; the mouse wheel will modify scale and pan, aiming to keep mouse position stable during zoom.


**Finalizing Event Handling**

I'm now implementing the event handling. I've successfully implemented:
-   `onWheel` for zooming
-   `onMouseDown`, `onMouseMove`, `onMouseUp` for selection and panning. 
-   Spacebar+drag panning works, with correct coordinate space transformations. 
-   The center-zoom implementation is working. 
-   Now I'm adding `App.tsx` integration and metadata checks. I have to think about the order of operations for generating image metadata for the service, and how it effects performance.

**Integrating Event Handling**

Now I'm tackling the integration. I've got zoom, pan, and selection working independently. My next step is to get the events wired up inside the ImageAnnotator component and flowing through to the rest of the application. I'm prioritizing getting those events handled well. I am working to keep the design clean, with clear responsibilities for each component. I anticipate needing to refine the interactions.


**Finalizing Image Masking**

I'm now implementing the masking in `canvasUtils.ts` to ensure compatibility with Gemini's rectangle requirement. For lasso and circle selections, I'm using a canvas to draw the shape, clip it, and draw the image.  This creates a rectangular image containing only the user's selected region.  I'm also considering default JPEG format to avoid alpha-channel issues in Gemini.  This is a critical step to ensure accurate analysis, regardless of selection shape.


**Updating Gemini Integration**

I'm now focusing on ensuring that Gemini receives appropriate image data regardless of the selected shape. I've updated the `canvasUtils.ts` to generate a rectangular image containing the masked selection, which should be compatible with the Gemini integration. The image type is now JPEG, to avoid potential alpha channel issues. I want to make sure I don't lose anything in translation.


**Standardizing Image Coordinates**

I'm solidifying coordinate spaces.  ImageAnnotator now returns coordinates relative to the original, *unscaled* image dimensions.  I'm using this base size because `img.width` won't update when using CSS transforms for zooming. I'll transform these for natural size before analysis.

The following changes add zoom, pan, and multi-shape selection (Rectangle, Circle, Lasso) to the ImageAnnotator.

# Updated files:

## types.ts
- Added `SelectionTool` type for 'RECTANGLE', 'CIRCLE', 'LASSO'.
- Added `SelectionShape` discriminated union to handle different selection data structures (rect/circle with geometry, lasso with points).
- Removed the standalone `Rect` interface usage in favor of `SelectionShape` where appropriate (though kept `Rect` for utility usage).

## utils/canvasUtils.ts
- Updated `cropImage` to accept `SelectionShape`. It now calculates the bounding box for the shape (handling Lasso points), sizes the canvas to that bounding box, applies a clipping path matching the shape (rect, ellipse, or polygon), and draws the image.
- Added `getSelectionBoundingBox` helper.
- Added `convertScreenSelectionToNatural` to scale any selection shape from screen coordinates to natural image coordinates.

## components/ImageAnnotator.tsx
- Implemented a transformation layer (`transform: translate(...) scale(...)`) for the image.
- Added a toolbar with icons for Tools (Pan, Rect, Circle, Lasso) and Zoom controls.
- Implemented logic to map mouse events from container space (screen) to image space (transformed).
- Handled drawing logic for all three shapes:
    - **Rectangle**: Click and drag diagonal.
    - **Circle**: Click and drag diagonal (bounding box style).
    - **Lasso**: Click and drag to draw freehand path.
- Added `Pan` mode (toggleable or via Spacebar).
- Used SVG for rendering the selection overlay which scales with the image.

## App.tsx
- Updated state to store `SelectionShape` instead of `Rect`.
- Updated `handleAnalyze` to use the new utility functions for cropping and coordinate conversion.

entries.description: Added SelectionTool and SelectionShape types to support multiple selection modes.
Updated cropping logic to handle shapes (Circle, Lasso) via clipping paths and coordinate conversion.
Added Zoom/Pan controls, toolbar, and logic for Rectangle, Circle, and Lasso selection tools.
Update state and handlers to support SelectionShape logic.

**Improving Image Interpretation**

I'm now adding instructions in `geminiService.ts` to refine the system's image understanding. I'm focusing on ensuring it can accurately estimate object volume and integrate those estimates for print cost and time calculations using the Bambu Lab P1S specifications. This builds on my earlier work where visual object interpretation was a key focus. The addition is crucial to the overall cost calculation accuracy.


**Improving Print Cost Calculations**

I'm currently updating the `geminiService.ts` file to improve the print cost calculations. I'm focusing on incorporating the visual input and providing instructions on calculating costs based on that visual data. I've broken down the steps into a more detailed process and am including instructions to generate a table with volume estimations.

To add the Cost & Time Calculator feature based on Bambu Lab P1S specifications, I will update the `geminiService.ts`. This will involve enriching the System Prompt with P1S technical data (speed, acceleration, material density) and instructing the AI to append a structured estimation section (Time, Weight, Cost) to its analysis.

# Updated files:

entries.description: Enriched System Prompt with Bambu Lab P1S specifications and added instructions to generate a Cost & Time Estimation table.

payload.text:
Add this feature : SystĂ¨me de Projets & "Workbook"

On ne travaille jamais sur une impression complexe en une seule requĂŞte.

Dossiers par Projet : CrĂ©er un projet "CathĂ©drale X" ou "PiĂ¨ce de rechange Y".

Persistance du contexte : L'IA se souvient de toutes les images et contraintes prĂ©cĂ©dentes du projet (ex: "Rappelle-toi, je t'ai dit que c'Ă©tait du PETG").

Versionning : Garder l'historique des itĂ©rations (v1 trop fragile, v2 renforcĂ©e).

**Initiating Feature Design**

I'm focusing now on the user's need for a "Project System & Workbook."  I've pinned down that users need to build projects, store their analysis sessions within them (images, prompts, advice), and let the AI maintain coherence across sessions. I'm exploring how best to structure the user interface for this, considering folder-like structures versus more dynamic tagging systems.


**Expanding Contextual Awareness**

I'm now fully immersed in the mechanics of context. The core challenge is how to maintain project-specific context within the AI's prompts, ensuring that it remembers prior constraints and iterative analysis steps.  I'm devising a method to package previous interactions into each prompt sent to the Gemini API, essentially crafting a dynamic history injection. I'm exploring the implications for localStorage and state management, as this context data can grow over time.


**Optimizing Storage Constraints**

I'm now refining the data structure for localStorage, realizing that storing base64 images within the Session objects is unsustainable due to the 5MB limit. I'll need to trim things down; I'll send textual history to the AI for context, and store only the bare essentials in session entries: user prompts, AI responses, and perhaps a thumbnail.


**Formalizing Data Structures**

I'm solidifying the data structures required for project management. I've designed `Project` and `ProjectSession` interfaces in `types.ts`, and I'll proceed with a "lightweight" storage method. This will involve the creation of a `ProjectSidebar` component and context injection that includes previous Q&A pairs. I'm moving toward updating the `analyzeImageZone` function to handle project histories.


**Developing Project UI**

I'm now focusing on integrating the project management UI. I'm building out a `ProjectSidebar` that lists existing projects and allows users to create, delete, and switch between them.  My mental scratchpad has turned into actual code for project service functions and the `App` component's state management.  The key is to connect the analysis process to a specific project.


**Addressing Image Persistence**

I've been grappling with the `localStorage` limitations and decided to optimize.  To keep the application responsive, I'm storing the textual history of interactions instead of base64 images within project objects. This ensures that previous prompts and AI responses are preserved for contextual awareness. The 'remember the images' aspect is achieved by keeping the current image in memory. The context sent to the AI is purely textual.


**Finalizing Integration Design**

I'm now integrating the project management logic with the existing codebase.  The core data structures (`Project`, `ProjectSession`) are in place, and a dedicated storage service is handling localStorage interactions. I'm focusing on the `geminiService` update to handle contextual project histories, which will be constructed within `App.tsx` from the active project's session data. I'm designing a user-friendly sidebar for project navigation and a dedicated history tab within the advice panel for reviewing past interactions.


**Integrating Project Histories**

I'm now integrating the project history into the codebase. I've designed the `App.tsx` component to handle project state and manage the loading and saving of projects to local storage. I'm focusing on the Gemini service update to accept project history and ensure contextual awareness when analyzing new images. I've broken down the steps into creating a `ProjectList` and adding the new `Project History` feature to the `AdvicePanel`.


**Developing Workbook Interface**

I'm now focused on the "Workbook" feature. I'm adding a "History" tab to the `AdvicePanel` component to toggle between the current analysis and the project's interactive history. I'm carefully managing `localStorage` by storing only the textual history of interactions (user prompts and AI responses), to maintain project context without exceeding storage limits. I've defined the `ProjectSession` and `Project` interfaces in `types.ts` to structure this data.


**Finalizing Project Integration**

I've been working on a significant overhaul of the codebase to fully integrate the project management features. I've updated the `geminiService.ts` and `App.tsx` files to include project selection and session management. I've designed a dedicated `ProjectManager` component to provide a user-friendly interface. Also, I've added a "Workbook" tab to the `AdvicePanel` component to allow for easy access to the project history. The core functionality is working now; time to test!

I will implement the Project System & "Workbook" feature. This requires establishing a persistent storage mechanism (using `localStorage`), managing project state, and injecting the conversation history into the Gemini context so the AI remembers previous constraints.

Here is the plan:

1.  **`types.ts`**: Define `Project` and `ProjectSession` interfaces.
2.  **`utils/storageUtils.ts`**: Create a utility to manage loading/saving projects from LocalStorage.
3.  **`services/geminiService.ts`**: Update the analysis function to accept a `historyContext` string, allowing the AI to see previous interactions within the project.
4.  **`components/ProjectManager.tsx`**: Create a new component to list, create, and select projects.
5.  **`components/AdvicePanel.tsx`**: Update to include a "Workbook" (History) tab so users can review previous iterations.
6.  **`App.tsx`**: Integrate the project state, handle saving sessions after analysis, and pass the project history to the AI.

entries.description: Added Project and ProjectSession interfaces for the workbook feature.
New utility file to handle Project persistence in localStorage.
Updated to include Project History in the prompt context.
New component to handle project list and creation.
Added Tabs for "Analysis" and "Workbook" (History).
Integrated Project Manager and logic to save analysis to active project.
